- CGIAR implemented capacity-building programs on climate-smart agriculture and climate information services (10568/179514).
- CGIAR mapped vulnerability of tree species to guide conservation and restoration efforts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
- CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
- CGIAR conducted training on attribution of climate change and its impacts (10568/178443).
- CGIAR analyzed biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
- CGIAR assessed economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
- CGIAR analyzed narratives and promises of 'avoided deforestation' implementation (https://doi.org/10.1505/146554820829403469).

• CGIAR built capacities of university lecturers in climate-smart agriculture technologies and climate information services (10568/179514).

• CGIAR conducted training on attribution of climate change and its impacts for African climate negotiators (10568/178443).
• CGIAR examined biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed two adaptation scenarios to climate change for Sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

• ICRAF and SUA developed fertilizer application rates for maize in the semi-arid zone of Tanzania to sustain soil fertility (doi:10.7910/DVN/6YPPQO).

rice.narrative: CGIAR centers prioritized rice research across adaptation, mitigation, and water management through various innovations. Specifically:
rice.Adaptation:
• IFPRI analyzed climate impact on rice yields under different emissions scenarios and climate models (doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
• IFPRI promoted drought- and flood-tolerant rice varieties and climate-smart agriculture practices in Mali (10568/179427).
• IFPRI explored future rice market segments and trait priorities to enhance climate resilience in Southeast Asia (10568/174900).
• IFPRI scaled digital advisory tools like RiceAdvice to support climate-smart rice production in Mali (10568/179425).
• IFPRI developed climate-related risk maps and adaptation plans for rice production in Vietnam’s Mekong River Delta (10568/90253).
• IFPRI examined climate-induced quality changes in temperate-grown rice and adaptive strategies (https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202505.2196.v1).
rice.Mitigation:
• IFPRI explored policies for low-emission rice production practices in Southeast Asia (10568/163419).
• IFPRI analyzed rice cultivation actions in Nationally Determined Contributions, highlighting mitigation potential (10568/116169).
• IFPRI proposed an investment plan for low-emission rice production using the AWD technique in Vietnam (10568/101315).
rice.Water:
• IFPRI identified water management as a key strategy for both mitigation and adaptation in rice production (10568/116169).
• IFPRI discussed water-efficient practices and precision agriculture for temperate rice production (https://doi.org/10.20944/preprints202505.2196.v1).
• IFPRI highlighted the System of Rice Intensification (SRI) and water management for climate-smart agriculture (10568/77040).

roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized adaptation strategies for roots and tubers through various innovations. Specifically:
- HarvestPlus and the International Potato Center (CIP) conducted the Reaching End Users (REU) project to distribute biofortified orange-fleshed sweet potato (OFSP) in Mozambique and Uganda (doi:10.7910/DVN/RN40SP).
- The International Potato Center developed climate-smart varieties of potatoes and sweetpotatoes that tolerate heat, drought, or saline conditions (10568/106939).
roots and tubers.Adaptation:
• HarvestPlus and the International Potato Center (CIP) implemented the Reaching End Users (REU) project to promote the adoption of biofortified orange-fleshed sweet potato (OFSP) in Mozambique and Uganda (doi:10.7910/DVN/RN40SP).
• The International Potato Center developed climate-smart varieties of potatoes and sweetpotatoes that tolerate heat, drought, or saline conditions (10568/106939).
• CIAT and Vietnamese partners conducted a study on the adoption of improved cassava varieties and assessed impacts on farm productivity and income in Vietnam (doi:10.7910/DVN/4HJJXZ).
• PepsiCo, in collaboration with CGIAR-affiliated researchers, developed climate-yield models and the Climate Resilience Platform to enhance supply-chain resilience in potato production across 12 countries (10568/152422).
• CGIAR researchers assessed climate change risks and opportunities in the potato sector in Kenya, highlighting the potential of climate-smart agriculture practices (10568/103233).
• CGIAR researchers conducted a climate risk assessment in Uganda, emphasizing the need for climate-smart agriculture practices to reduce economic losses and build resilience in the potato sector (10568/107721).
• CIAT systematically evaluated cassava germplasm accessions for adaptation to different soils and climates, resistance to pests and diseases, and quality traits (doi:10.7910/DVN/KM8CQG).
roots and tubers.Mitigation: No evidence found in this sample.
roots and tubers.Water: • CIAT and partners conducted suitability mapping for cassava in the Mekong Delta, Cambodia, considering irrigation and other management practices to identify optimal growing locations and seasons (10568/173187).

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR centers prioritized wheat research through various innovations in adaptation and mitigation. Specifically:
- IFPRI assessed the effects of COVID-19 lockdowns on wheat farmers' crop losses and food security (doi:10.7910/DVN/PAWM4U).
- Bioversity International and CIAT analyzed climate change-related vulnerabilities and adaptation strategies for wheat value chains (10568/111337).
wheat.Adaptation:
• IFPRI assessed the effects of COVID-19 lockdowns on wheat farmers' crop losses, harvesting practices, and food security (doi:10.7910/DVN/PAWM4U).
• IFPRI, Ghent University, and University of Manchester developed smartphone-based near-surface remote sensing to monitor wheat growth and production risks (doi:10.7910/DVN/DBAFZY).
• Bioversity International and CIAT analyzed climate change-related vulnerabilities and adaptation strategies for wheat value chains (10568/111337).
• Bioversity International and CIAT explored policy issues and recommendations for enhancing access to agricultural genetic resources for climate-change adaptation in East Africa (10568/116203).
• Ambaw, Tadesse, and Recha implemented climate-smart agriculture practices to enhance farmer resilience and crop productivity in Ethiopia (10568/106308).
• van Etten et al. tested crowdsourced citizen science to improve variety recommendations for wheat in India (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116).
wheat.Mitigation: • Tadesse et al. investigated the effects of climate-smart agriculture on soil fertility, crop yield, and soil carbon sequestration in Southern Ethiopia (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515).
wheat.Water: No evidence found in this sample.
